data.title: Molina signals 2026 premium revenue of ~$42B and at least $5 EPS while lifting Medicaid attrition outlook to 6%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Molina Healthcare (MOH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Our full year 2026 guidance, which we reaffirm at approximately $42 billion of premium revenue and at least $5 in adjusted earnings per share" (President, CEO & Director Joseph Zubretsky).
  • "Last night, we reported adjusted earnings per share of $2.35 on $10.2 billion of premium revenue" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky), alongside "Our 91.1% consolidated MCR" and "a 1.6% adjusted pretax margin" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).
  • "We feel confident in our 5% medical cost trend assumption for 2026" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky), after noting that in 2025 the company "observed a 7.5% medical cost trend that included 250 basis points of acuity shift related to the post-pandemic redetermination process" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).
  • "Our Duals business is the strategic focus for us in Medicare" and "we will exit the MAPD product for 2027" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).
  • "Given the pricing actions we took in our Marketplace segment this year, we have reduced our exposure and prioritized margin improvement" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Mark Keim), and "Renewing members now represent 70% of our book" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).

Outlook

  • "We are merely reaffirming our full year 2026 adjusted earnings per share guidance of at least $5" and "Our full year 2026 premium revenue guidance remains at approximately $42 billion" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).
  • "We note that our forecast for Medicaid membership attrition increased slightly" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky); CFO Mark Keim specified, "We now expect same-store membership in Medicaid to decline 6% this year, up from previous guidance of a 2% decline" and "We expect to end the year with approximately 4.5 million members" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • "With normal market attrition, we expect membership in our Marketplace segment to end the year at approximately 250,000" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • "Several of our States have already provided off-cycle rate increases, and these would represent upside to our guidance" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • "When we report second quarter results, we will update our full year 2026 guidance" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky), adding "we are not updating it at Investor Day" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).

Financial Results

  • "For the quarter, we reported approximately $10.2 billion of premium revenue with adjusted EPS of $2.35" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • Segment cost performance was framed through medical ratios: "In Medicaid, our first quarter reported MCR was 92%"; "In Medicare, our first quarter reported MCR was 89.8%"; and "In Marketplace, our first quarter reported MCR was 84%" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • Cash and capital were emphasized: "Our operating cash flow for the quarter was $1.1 billion" and "our parent company cash balance was approximately $213 million at the end of the quarter" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • Leverage and reserves were discussed: "Debt at the end of the quarter was 6.1x trailing 12-month EBITDA, and our debt-to-cap ratio was about 48" and "Days in claims payable at the end of the quarter was 44" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).

Q&A

  • Andrew Mok, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: "which states are driving that incremental pressure" on Medicaid attrition? President, CEO & Director Zubretsky: "We underestimated the impact in California, Illinois and New York and somewhat in Texas"; Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim: "In our new guidance, it's now 6%" and "we don't really see an acuity impact here."
  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho Securities USA LLC, Research Division: "What are you expecting for 2026?" on cash flow and "What is the goal?" on debt-to-cap? Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim: "My cash at the parent is a little over $200 million in the first quarter. It will be more than $600 million at the end of the year" and "Typically, we target something in the low 40s" for debt-to-cap.
  • Kevin Fischbeck, BofA Securities, Research Division: is guidance caution just timing, or "meaningful unknowns"? President, CEO & Director Zubretsky: "Our prudent move to not increase guidance at the first quarter" reflects Medicaid cost volatility, Marketplace seasonality checks, and that Medicare integrated products are "1 quarter" into launch.
  • Sarah James, Cantor Fitzgerald & Co., Research Division: asked about Florida reserves and RBC; President, CEO & Director Zubretsky: "We believe that total run rate is a $6 billion revenue program" for Florida CMS; Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim: "We only take dividends when they are above the RBC target of 300."
  • Scott Fidel, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: requested Medicare run-rate clarity with MAPD exit; President, CEO & Director Zubretsky: MAPD is "producing $1 earnings per share drag"; Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim: "the MAPD component of that is $1 loss on $1.2 billion of revenue" and duals run rate is "about $5.5 billion" with "about a 94% MLR."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on Medicaid attrition, utilization mix, and guidance conservatism, including "meaningful unknowns" (Kevin Fischbeck, BofA Securities, Research Division) and repeated probing on "low and no utilizers" (Stephen Baxter, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division; Hua Ha, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive but cautious, repeatedly framing decisions as prudence: "merely reaffirming our prior full year guidance is a prudent approach" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky) and "we use the term time tested" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s downside shock language ("fell well below our expectations" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky) in Q4 2025), Q1 2026 language emphasized stability and cleaner execution: "The quarter was clean" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The quarter shifted from Q4 2025’s earnings miss ("an adjusted loss per share of $2.75" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky) tied to "unexpected retroactive premium rate actions" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky)) to Q1 2026 profitability and reaffirmation: "adjusted earnings per share of $2.35" and guidance reaffirmed at "approximately $42 billion" premium revenue and "at least $5" EPS (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).
  • Guidance language stayed cautious, but key assumptions moved: Q4 2025 guided Medicaid attrition as "2%" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky) while Q1 2026 revised to "same-store membership in Medicaid to decline 6% this year" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim), paired with "we don't really see an acuity impact here" (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • Analysts’ focus rotated from Q4 2025’s retro Medicaid items and Marketplace disruption to Q1 2026’s Medicaid enrollment mechanics, Medicare duals transition performance, and Florida CMS implementation economics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Medicaid enrollment and policy administration risk: "States continue to evaluate their processes and how to implement work requirements and biannual redeterminations" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky), and "it is still a bit unclear" how termination and exceptions will work for community engagement requirements (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).
  • Medicaid membership volatility by state: "California, Illinois, New York, Texas" were cited as driving higher attrition assumptions (Senior EVP, CFO & Treasurer Keim).
  • Marketplace regulatory uncertainty: "as we approach the 2027 pricing cycle, we will likely remain cautious as it is still possible for disruptive regulatory changes to occur" (President, CEO & Director Zubretsky).

Final Takeaway

Molina’s management described Q1 2026 as operationally steady, reporting $2.35 in adjusted EPS on $10.2 billion of premium revenue while reaffirming full-year 2026 guidance of approximately $42 billion in premium revenue and at least $5 in adjusted EPS. The biggest modeling change versus the prior quarter was a higher Medicaid same-store attrition outlook to 6% (from 2%), with management repeatedly asserting that low/no-utilizer mix shifts seen during redeterminations are “largely behind us,” supporting confidence in the company’s 5% medical cost trend assumption for 2026. Management also reiterated its Medicare strategic shift—"focus exclusively on dual eligible members"—including a planned exit of traditional MAPD for 2027, while pointing investors to the May 8 Investor Day for a detailed outlook through 2029.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: United Rentals signals 2026 free cash flow of $2.15B-$2.45B while lifting revenue outlook to $16.9B-$17.4B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: United Rentals (URI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Matthew Flannery said the company posted “a strong start to 2026, including first quarter records across revenue, EBITDA and EPS,” and added that “the momentum we're carrying into our busy season…supports our expectations that this will be another record year, as further evidenced by our updated guidance.”
  • Flannery highlighted growth drivers as “healthy growth across both our gen rent and specialty businesses,” with specialty “up 14% year-over-year… and opened 17 cold starts,” and said activity included “data centers” along with “health care, infrastructure, power [and] industrial manufacturing.”
  • Flannery said used equipment demand remained supportive: “We sold $680 million of OEC at a 51% recovery rate” and “we're on track to sell approximately $2.8 billion of fleet this year.”
  • CFO William Grace said, “we're pleased to be raising our full year guidance based on the momentum we're carrying into our busy season and strong customer sentiment,” and noted “we recorded $45 million of restructuring charges… primarily related to the consolidation of overlapping facilities and headcount reductions.”

Outlook

  • Grace raised full-year expectations, saying total revenue is “now expected in the range of $16.9 billion to $17.4 billion,” adjusted EBITDA is “$7.625 billion to $7.875 billion,” gross CapEx is “$4.4 billion to $4.8 billion,” and free cash flow is “$2.15 billion to $2.45 billion.”
  • Grace reiterated shareholder return plans: “it remains our plan to repurchase $1.5 billion of shares in 2026,” and said buybacks plus dividends would “return roughly $2 billion to our shareholders this year.”
  • Versus the prior call, management increased the full-year revenue range by $100 million, increased adjusted EBITDA by $50 million, and increased gross CapEx by $100 million, while keeping used-sales expectations at “around $1.45 billion.”

Financial Results

  • Flannery reported “total revenue grew by 7% year-over-year to nearly $4 billion,” with “rental revenue… almost 9% to $3.4 billion,” and said “adjusted EPS came in at $9.71.”
  • Grace said “rental revenue increased $274 million… or 8.7%… supported primarily by growth from large projects and key verticals,” and added “ancillary and re-rent grew by nearly 18%, adding a combined $111 million.”
  • Grace said first-quarter adjusted EBITDA margin was “44.1%,” and explained underlying cost actions, including “steps across the organization to control variable costs with a significant focus on labor and outside hauling.”
  • Grace said liquidity was “almost $3.4 billion,” net leverage was “1.9x,” and capital returns in the quarter were “$500 million… $125 million via dividends and $375 million through repurchases.”

Q&A

  • David Raso, Evercore ISI: asked why guidance implies lower incremental margins after Q1 cost savings; Executive VP & CFO Grace: “we caution people against anchoring to the midpoint… we've got to sustain that through the busy season,” with delivery “the most important to focus on.”
  • Raso, Evercore ISI: asked about fleet productivity cadence; CEO Flannery: “the supply-demand dynamics… are conducive to driving positive fleet productivity,” and said Q4 mix issues were “an anomaly.”
  • Robert Wertheimer, Melius: asked about timing from discussions to project planning and dirt as a leading indicator; CEO Flannery: “we have more visibility on those large projects,” adding on dirt, “we're seeing strength across our portfolio.”
  • Michael Feniger, BofA: asked about ancillary contribution, repositioning, and fuel; CFO Grace: ancillary contribution margin “very much in line with that 20%,” delivery improved “about 10 or 15 basis points,” and fuel is managed via “pass-through… [and] an active hedging program.”
  • Feniger, BofA: asked about gen rent competition; CEO Flannery: “there's always somebody that wants what you have… we spend a lot of time building a competitive moat.”
  • Steven Fisher, UBS: asked about sustaining delivery performance and inflation offsets; CEO Flannery: “we're not going to eliminate… repositioning… [but] mitigate it,” while CFO Grace said inflation “has played out as expected.”
  • Fisher, UBS: asked about facility closures trade-offs; CEO Flannery: “we didn't exit any markets… 95% plus of our equipment is delivered,” and said closures were “specific and surgical.”
  • Jerry Revich, Wells Fargo: asked about dollar utilization and demand cadence; CEO Flannery: “we still focus on it relentlessly at the branch level,” while CFO Grace pointed to “strong fleet productivity.”
  • Kenneth Newman, KeyBanc: asked about supplier surcharges and flexing fleet purchases; CEO Flannery: “we do lock in our prices for the year,” and on surcharges, “we're not worried about that.”
  • Newman, KeyBanc: asked about M&A; CEO Flannery: “the deal pipelines remain pretty consistent,” and said the company would “lean towards specialty.”
  • Kyle Menges, Citi: asked about local markets and uncertainty; CEO Flannery: “the local market continues to be stable.”
  • Menges, Citi: asked about OEM dealers expanding rental; CEO Flannery: “really not much overlap there.”
  • Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley: asked about recent acquisitions’ impact; CEO Flannery said Q1 spend was “about $400 million… already embedded in our guidance,” and CFO Grace called dollar utilization impact “very de minimis.”
  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: asked about World Cup tailwind and CapEx mix; CEO Flannery: “I wouldn't model anything extra for the World Cup… it's already been embedded in our guidance,” and said the $100 million CapEx increase is “across the portfolio.”
  • Timothy Thein, Raymond James: asked about delivery cost recovery vs prior spikes; CFO Grace cited 2022 as an analog where diesel rose “over 50%,” but fuel impact was “like a 15 basis point increase.”
  • Thein, Raymond James: asked about specialty asset efficiency; CFO Grace: “Truthfully, I'll need to come back to you on that.”
  • Jamie Cook, Truist: asked about gen rent margin sustainability and matting timing; CFO Grace said focus is “sustaining… through the busy part of the season,” and said matting “ended up it will be 2Q.”
  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson: asked what’s driving time utilization; CEO Flannery: “it's everything… having the right fleet in the right places.”
  • Scott Schneeberger, Oppenheimer: asked about branch closures and cold starts, plus smaller customers; CEO Flannery said closures were “split pretty much across the portfolio,” and smaller customers are “stable.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on sustainability of margins, delivery/repositioning costs, fuel exposure, and competitiveness, including Raso’s question on whether Q1 margin strength was “a little bit of an anomaly.”
  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident in demand and execution, with repeated emphasis on discipline and mitigation, including Flannery saying “we feel confident we're positioned to win in the marketplace” and Grace saying the team is “incredibly focused.”
  • Compared with last quarter’s heavier focus on repositioning headwinds and matting-related mix, the current call sounded more confident on execution, with management pointing to early-year cost actions and a higher full-year outlook.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted higher versus Q4’s initial 2026 ranges, with current management explicitly saying the year is “playing out better than we expected just a few months ago.”
  • Cost narrative evolved from Q4’s emphasis on repositioning as a margin headwind to Q1’s emphasis on actions taken, including “consolidation of overlapping facilities,” and tighter management of “labor and outside hauling.”
  • Analyst focus remained consistent on delivery/repositioning and fleet productivity mechanics, but Q1 questions leaned more into why full-year margin implications appear more conservative after a strong Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Grace flagged ongoing operational sensitivity to peak-season execution: “we've got to sustain that through the busy season,” with delivery identified as the key risk area.
  • Management highlighted fuel as a monitored variable, with Grace saying exposure is largely “pass-through” and the remainder managed via “an active hedging program.”
  • Thein’s specialty efficiency question surfaced an unresolved detail, with Grace saying, “I'll need to come back to you on that.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a record start, tied to large-project momentum and broad specialty growth, and raised full-year revenue, adjusted EBITDA and CapEx ranges while keeping the share repurchase plan intact. Across Q&A, analysts repeatedly tested whether margin performance can hold through the busy season amid delivery/repositioning complexity and fuel volatility, while management emphasized mitigation processes, disciplined cost control, and confidence in sustained positive fleet productivity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Huntington Bancshares upgraded to Neutral at Piper as positives increase
data.description:
Piper Sandler upgraded Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) to neutral from Underweight after the company announced increased stock buybacks and its Q1 results featured an intensified focus on cost control.

Huntington Bancshares (HBAN), undergoing integration of two sizeable acquisitions, posted Q1 earnings that exceeded the Wall Street consensus on Thursday as its noninterest income topped consensus.

Core fees were a highlight of the Q1 results, with several lines beating Piper's expectations.

Analyst R. Scott Siefers pointed to the $3B repurchase authorization, "a heavier scrutiny on cost control," and that "recently outsized loan growth is getting 'tuned' down to reflect macro uncertainty."

"Plus, with the shares having underperformed so much over the past year, the risk-reward seems to be coming into much better balance," he wrote in a note to clients.

That means a Neutral rating is more appropriate, he added.

The rating aligns with the SA Quant rating of Hold and contrasts with the average Wall Street rating of Buy.

data.title: WEX forecasts $2.82B-$2.88B 2026 revenue and $18.95-$19.55 adjusted EPS, while planning $50M in cost-saving actions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WEX (WEX) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Melissa Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) said, “The first quarter marked a strong start to the year for WEX,” adding, “We exceeded the high end of our guidance range for both revenue and adjusted net income per diluted share.”
  • Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) highlighted an AI-driven efficiency and reinvestment framework: “In 2026, we plan to deliver $50 million in cost-saving actions, including savings from automation and modernization with a portion of the proceeds to be reinvested in the business and the remainder to flow through to margins.”
  • On Mobility, Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) emphasized execution despite market pressure: “Payment processing transactions were down 3%, so this is not a story of the market suddenly snapping back. Rather, it's a story of improving execution.” She also said, “We are closely monitoring energy price volatility related to the Middle East conflict.”
  • On Corporate Payments, Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) announced, “We entered into a long-term renewal with a large and strategically important travel customer,” and added, “the economics of the renewal are already contemplated in our guidance and are fully reflected in our Q1 results.”
  • Jagtar Narula (Chief Financial Officer) framed the quarter as execution plus macro noise: “Overall, this was a very noisy quarter in the macro. But the real story is a solid performance across the business that is positioning us well for the remainder of 2026.”

Outlook

  • Narula (Chief Financial Officer) guided Q2 revenue to $727 million to $747 million and adjusted net income EPS to $4.93 to $5.13.
  • Narula (Chief Financial Officer) raised full-year guidance to revenue of $2.82 billion to $2.88 billion and adjusted net income EPS of $18.95 to $19.55, stating, “Compared to the midpoints of the previous ranges, these represent increases of $120 million in revenue and $1.70 in EPS.”
  • Narula (Chief Financial Officer) attributed the guide change primarily to fuel assumptions: “You should think of these increases as largely driven by updating our fuel price assumption to $4.30 per gallon in Q2 and $3.70 per gallon for the full year.”
  • Narula (Chief Financial Officer) also updated rate assumptions: “We are no longer assuming any rate cuts for the rest of the year in guidance,” and said the impact to full-year guidance was “immaterial.”

Financial results

  • The company reported Q1 revenue of $673.8 million and adjusted EPS of $4.15. Narula (Chief Financial Officer) said Q1 revenue was “above the top end of the guidance range we provided last quarter.”
  • Narula (Chief Financial Officer) detailed unusual fuel/FX dynamics, including “an unfavorable $7.6 million revenue impact” from European spread movements, which offset “the favorable $5.5 million revenue impact from U.S. fuel prices,” and said the quarter also included “a favorable $5.1 million revenue impact from FX gains.”
  • Segment results disclosed on the call included Benefits revenue of $216.2 million and Corporate Payments revenue of $113 million. Narula (Chief Financial Officer) also reported Corporate Payments travel-related revenue “grew approximately 12%,” while “revenue from non-travel customers grew in the mid-single digits.”
  • On margins, Narula (Chief Financial Officer) said Q1 adjusted operating income margin “declined 50 basis points,” driven primarily by “an increase in credit losses from 12 basis points to 19 basis points.” He added, “For 2026, we are expecting margin expansion of approximately 75 basis points on a macro-neutral basis.”
  • Narula (Chief Financial Officer) reported trailing 12-month adjusted free cash flow of $671 million and said leverage ended Q1 at 3.1x, with the company “on trajectory to reach the midpoint of our leverage range in the second half of the year.”

Q&A

  • David Koning, Baird: asked whether Mobility growth could accelerate after a strong Q1; Melissa Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) said the company “held the rest of the year to what we had previously guided” to “be cautious,” while adding, “we have a number of really positive things that are going our way right now.”
  • David Koning, Baird: asked why Mobility EBIT was down; Jagtar Narula (Chief Financial Officer) said year-over-year pressure came from “sales and marketing and credit losses,” tied to “new offers” that were pulled, with credit-loss roll-through impacting margin.
  • Ramsey El-Assal, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about higher fuel prices and credit/spread volatility; Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) said, “we're not seeing it impact credit quality,” and explained Europe spread impacts occur when “rapid changes in prices” hit.
  • Mihir Bhatia, BofA: asked about Mobility organic growth and processing rate outlook; Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) cited “new customers,” “retention,” and that “pricing has had an impact,” while Narula (Chief Financial Officer) said processing rates should be “roughly remain flat to the first quarter” and “start to rise again” later as fuel prices decline.
  • Christopher Svensson, Deutsche Bank: pressed on Mobility pricing; Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) said, “We had about $70 million worth of pricing actions that we took in '24 and '25,” and described balancing “customer attrition” against price moves.
  • Svensson, Deutsche Bank: asked about travel softness; Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) said weakness was “Middle East travel,” adding, “It's in the order of about $3 million a quarter for us,” and said it was reflected in Q2 guidance.
  • Daniel Krebs, Wolfe Research: suggested the full-year raise only reflected fuel; Narula (Chief Financial Officer) responded, “that's actually incorrect. We passed through the first quarter beat as well as changes in fuel prices, FX, interest rates.”
  • Michael Infante, Morgan Stanley: asked about Benefits deposit economics; Narula (Chief Financial Officer) said moving deposits to WEX Bank typically yields “about 50 to 100 basis points better,” adding there is “$400 million-ish” still at third-party banks but “a lot of that is used for operational purposes.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on how much of the upside was sustainable (Mobility acceleration, spreads, take rates, and whether the guide captured the quarter); the sharpest pushback was Krebs’ “that's actually incorrect” setup on the EPS raise.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on macro/travel. Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) emphasized prudence: “we just want to be cautious about it,” while also reiterating confidence in execution and pricing levers.
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on an “inflection point” and scaling, this quarter added more explicit near-term caution around volatility (fuel spreads, Middle East travel) while maintaining the scaling narrative through cost saves and margin framework.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance shifted materially higher in Q1 2026 versus Q4 2025, with Narula (Chief Financial Officer) explicitly tying the change to fuel assumptions and removing anticipated rate cuts.
  • Corporate Payments commentary evolved from Q4’s focus on travel strength and normalized visibility to Q1’s narrower concern: Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) cited isolated Middle East softness and said it was included in Q2 guidance.
  • Benefits operational decisions were more prominent in Q1: Narula (Chief Financial Officer) said SaaS account growth was “slightly lower than what we guided” due to “shutting down a noncore product,” calling the revenue/income impact “immaterial.”
  • Capital allocation messaging tightened: Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) reiterated a pivot “from accretive M&A to share repurchases,” while specifying, “we are prioritizing debt reduction until our leverage ratio is below 3x.”

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited fuel-price volatility and European spread dynamics as a key uncertainty, with Narula (Chief Financial Officer) describing the quarter’s fuel sensitivity as “less accurate” due to diesel/gasoline divergence and late-fee timing.
  • Travel demand risk was discussed as region-specific: Smith (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO) said softness was concentrated in the Middle East corridor and quantified exposure at “about $3 million a quarter.”
  • Credit losses remained a watched item, with Narula (Chief Financial Officer) attributing prior-period offers to elevated losses rolling through results and emphasizing those offers were removed.

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 as broad-based execution with notable macro “noise,” raised full-year revenue and adjusted EPS guidance primarily on updated fuel assumptions, and kept the core strategy centered on scaling AI-driven productivity, disciplined pricing, and capital allocation that prioritizes sub-3x leverage. They characterized Mobility improvement as execution-led despite transactions being down, disclosed a long-term travel renewal already embedded in results and guidance, and flagged a narrow Middle East travel softness in April that was reflected in Q2 expectations.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CenterPoint reiterates 2026 non-GAAP EPS $1.89-$1.91 as Houston Electric firmly committed load rises to 12.2 GW
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CenterPoint Energy (CNP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “On today's call, I'd like to address 4 key areas of focus for the quarter,” including “yet another significant increase in our firmly committed load forecast to 12.2 gigawatts of new industrial load,” and “growing optimism for transformational load growth opportunities for our Indiana Electric service territory.” (President, CEO & Chairman Jason Wells)

  • “This morning, we reported non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 for the first quarter of 2026,” and “we are reiterating our full year 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance of $1.89 to $1.91.” (President, CEO & Chairman Wells)

  • “We now have clear line of sight to 12.2 gigawatts of firmly committed load,” with “ERCOT approval for 3.2 gigawatts of this load,” and the company expecting “to submit the remaining 9 gigawatts of projects to ERCOT for approval within the next few weeks.” (President, CEO & Chairman Wells)

  • “We invested $1.2 billion in the first quarter” and “remain firmly on track to execute the $6.8 billion of planned work this year,” while noting the Ohio LDC sale is “on track to close in the fourth quarter of this year.” (Executive VP & CFO Christopher Foster)

Outlook

  • “We are reiterating our full year 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance of $1.89 to $1.91,” and “over the long term, we continue to expect to grow non-GAAP EPS at the mid- to high end of our 7% to 9% annual guidance range through 2028 and 7% to 9% annually thereafter through 2035.” (President, CEO & Chairman Wells)

  • “Beyond our base 10-year $65.5 billion plan, we will continue to fold in the over $10 billion of incremental capital investment opportunities,” including potential additional investments as the company refreshes transmission planning, which it is “targeting to complete in the second half of this year.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management repeated the same 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance range and again pointed to incremental capital upside, while updating Houston’s “firmly committed load” to “12.2 gigawatts.” (President, CEO & Chairman Wells)

Financial Results

  • “On a GAAP EPS basis, we reported $0.48 for the first quarter of 2026,” and “on a non-GAAP EPS basis, we reported $0.56 for the quarter.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

  • “Growth in rate recovery contributed $0.11 when compared to the same quarter last year,” while “higher interest expense was $0.04 unfavorable,” and “the absence of earnings from our Louisiana and Mississippi businesses post divestiture resulted in $0.05 of unfavorability.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

  • “Our non-GAAP EPS excludes the impacts from the tax gain and other expenses related to the sale of our Ohio LDC,” and the company expects to start marketing its “temporary generation units for either a sublease or sale later this year.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

  • “As of the end of the first quarter, our adjusted FFO to debt ratio based on Moody's rating methodology was 12.5%,” and “we have completed nearly 70% of our planned 2026 financing needs.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: Asked what portion of Houston’s committed load is embedded in the plan vs. incremental upside and gating items; President, CEO & Chairman Wells said, “I wouldn't think about the 12 gigawatts of firmly committed load is directly driving CapEx,” adding, “for every 1 gigawatt of industrial load that we add to our system, it's about $6 million a month of incremental demand charges.”

  • Steven Fleishman, Wolfe Research: Asked about Indiana large-load investments and timing; President, CEO & Chairman Wells said, “All of that unlocks at least 1.5 gigawatts of incremental capacity,” and “I would think about this as more around about $1 billion opportunity,” with the timing “within '27, '28, '29.”

  • Richard Sunderland, Truist: Pressed on incremental transmission dollars this decade and the upcoming study; President, CEO & Chairman Wells said the study “seeks to kind of fill a gap around '29, '30 and '31,” and “it should be a fairly significant set of new transmission projects that we'll be able to highlight in the second half of this year.”

  • Richard Sunderland, Truist: Asked about MobileGen market opportunity; Executive VP & CFO Foster said the company is “already seeing very strong market receptivity,” with “directionally almost double the original lease rates,” and the “back-end date” to market larger units would be “probably the end of March 2027.”

  • Jeremy Tonet, JPMorgan: Asked about credit metric trajectory and year-end positioning; Executive VP & CFO Foster said, “we will end the year at the high end of the cushion,” citing pulled-forward debt issuance and AMT-related cash-tax changes, adding, “we'll have the opportunity to no longer be a cash taxpayer.”

  • Anthony Crowdell, Mizuho: Asked whether Houston’s “8 gigawatts” timeline slipped vs. prior materials; Executive VP & CFO Foster said, “It's the prior quarter, we had talked about 7.5. That number is now going to 8, and it's by the end of 2028 is the way to think about it.”

  • Andrew Weisel, Scotiabank: Asked whether AMT refunds could reduce the $4 billion common equity assumption and whether the convert changes equity needs; Executive VP & CFO Foster said, “that can provide us the equivalent of adding an incremental $1 billion of CapEx to the plan with no incremental equity.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly skeptical and detail-seeking, concentrating on what’s “embedded” vs. “incremental upside,” transmission timing, and balance-sheet flexibility tied to taxes and equity needs.

  • Management sentiment was positive and execution-focused, repeatedly emphasizing speed and visibility; President, CEO & Chairman Wells said, “we now have clear line of sight to 12.2 gigawatts of firmly committed load,” while Executive VP & CFO Foster said, “we are confident in our ability to execute.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, the tone remained similarly confident on guidance and long-term growth, with Q1 questions shifting toward quantifying incremental demand-charge economics and clarifying ERCOT batching/approval mechanics.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Houston load narrative tightened from Q4’s “2.5 gigawatts…in the construction phase” plus “5 gigawatts…firmly committed” to Q1’s “12.2 gigawatts of firmly committed load,” including “ERCOT approval for 3.2 gigawatts” and near-term plans to file “the remaining 9 gigawatts.” (President, CEO & Chairman Wells)

  • Capital-plan framing evolved from Q4’s explicit “$500 million” plan increase tied to an added 765 kV line to Q1’s emphasis on incremental transmission projects that will be detailed “later this year,” alongside “over $10 billion of incremental capital investment opportunities.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

  • Financing commentary progressed from Q4’s 13.8% FFO-to-debt discussion and AMT expectations to Q1’s “12.5%” (timing-related) and “nearly 70%” completion of 2026 financing needs. (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

Risks and Concerns

  • “We expect to submit the remaining 9 gigawatts of projects to ERCOT for approval within the next few weeks,” making ERCOT process timing a near-term execution dependency; management highlighted prior approvals “within less than 80 days of filing.” (President, CEO & Chairman Wells)

  • “Higher interest expense was $0.04 unfavorable,” though management pointed to the “$650 million convertible debt issuances” and reduced floating-rate exposure, plus “commercial paper balance…was 0.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

  • The Ohio LDC divestiture timing and related adjustments remained in focus, with non-GAAP results excluding items tied to a transaction that is “on track to close in the fourth quarter of this year.” (Executive VP & CFO Foster)

Final Takeaway

CenterPoint’s management emphasized first-quarter execution while holding full-year 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance at $1.89 to $1.91, and put the most investor-relevant emphasis on Houston Electric’s sharply higher “12.2 gigawatts” of firmly committed load, near-term ERCOT filings, and a demand-charge tailwind tied to industrial interconnections. Management also pointed to pending transmission planning updates in the second half of 2026 as the mechanism for converting near-term load acceleration into incremental wires investment, while highlighting a derisked financing posture with nearly 70% of 2026 needs completed and additional flexibility tied to cash-tax changes.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dime outlines path to over 3.50% NIM by Q4 2027 while lifting 2026 core expense outlook to about $260M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dime Community Bancshares (DCOM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "EPS for the first quarter was up 67% versus the prior year" and management tied the quarter to "record total core revenues of $124 million" and a "NIM" that "was up 10 basis points quarter-over-quarter as we were able to lower our cost of deposits" (CEO, President & Director Stuart Lubow).

  • "Our loan pipeline continues to be strong and is in excess of $1.5 billion with a weighted average rate of between 6.25% and 6.5%" and the company emphasized continued rotation in the loan mix: "growing business loans and managing the CRE ratio lower" (CEO Lubow).

  • "We are confident that these hires will be accretive to earnings in 2027" and Dime highlighted recruiting, including "2 very strong deposit teams" from the former Signature Bank and a new business line: "we will be adding a new equipment and franchise finance vertical starting May 1" (CEO Lubow).

  • "Earlier in this year, we announced plans to rebrand Dime at Dime Commercial Bank" and management framed it as reflecting the current franchise: "Over 70% of our deposit base is from commercial and municipal customers" (CEO Lubow).

  • "EPS for the first quarter was $0.75 per share" and "Core pretax pre-provision net revenue of $60.5 million represented 162 basis points of average assets" (COO, Senior EVP & CFO Avinash Reddy).

Outlook

  • "We expect modest NIM expansion in the second quarter and more pronounced NIM expansion in the back half of the year and in 2027 as the pace of the back book loan repricing picks up" (COO Reddy).

  • "We are increasing the expense guidance for core cash operating expenses, excluding intangible amortization for the full year to approximately $260 million" (COO Reddy).

  • "We expect to continue to reduce our CRE concentration ratio lower to 350% sometime between the second and third quarter of this year" (COO Reddy).

  • "Finally, we expect the tax rate for the remaining quarters of 2026 to be 28.5%" (COO Reddy).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guidance framework, management reiterated the timing emphasis on NIM: "more pronounced NIM expansion in the back half of the year" (COO Reddy) vs. "more substantial NIM expansion in the back half of the year" (COO Reddy, Q4 2025).

Financial Results

  • $0.75 EPS vs. $0.77 (analysts’ estimate); $124M total core revenues vs. $122,033,250 (analysts’ estimate).

  • "The reported first quarter NIM increased to 3.21%" and management added a modeling caveat: "Excluding the impact of the day count and the benefits from purchase accounting, the run rate NIM for the first quarter would have been closer to 3.14%" (COO Reddy).

  • "The NIM expansion versus the prior quarter was driven by a reduction in deposit costs to 1.70%" and Dime said "average earning assets for the month of March was approximately $14 billion, which should serve as a good base for modeling purposes going forward" (COO Reddy).

  • Credit and capital updates included: "The loan loss provision was approximately $12 million" and "our tangible equity ratio crossed 9%" alongside "Our common equity Tier 1 ratio grew to 11.87%" (COO Reddy).

  • Dime highlighted a loan disposition and expected P&L impact: "we transferred 4 loans totaling $38 million into held-for-sale status" and "we successfully sold these loans earlier this week, generating $36 million in total proceeds" with "a modest $2 million negative impact" expected in Q2 in the gain-on-sale line (COO Reddy).

Q&A

  • David Konrad, KBW: Asked whether the $38M sold loan was already in nonaccruals and about nonaccrual flow; COO Reddy: "it wasn't" and "that was new at March 31," adding "we have the pre-provision earnings power of the bank, the capital to offload relationships and credits where we don't think it meets our long-term objectives."

  • David Konrad, KBW: Asked when total loan growth inflects given CRE runoff; CEO Lubow: "towards the back 6 months of this year, you'll start to see nice growth on the loan portfolio" while COO Reddy outlined the mix and concluded "it should be mid-single-digit growth starting in the third quarter of this year."

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked about the Signature deposit teams’ historical book; CEO Lubow: "these were significant teams that we've been talking to for 3 years" and COO Reddy said current hires "manage well north of $1 billion of deposits" and framed "a $1 billion opportunity for us" over time.

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked what equipment finance will target; Chief Commercial Officer Thomas Geisel: "middle market to large ticket equipment finance deals" focused on "critical machinery and equipment for manufacturing and warehouses" and categories including "material handling" and "medical" among others.

  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: Pressed on additional deposit-cost improvement; COO Reddy: "it's going to be challenging for any bank to continue to drop deposit costs" if "the Fed is going to stay steady," but also said the deposit teams are "very DDA-heavy" and "should aid in the cost of funds overall."

  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: Asked about the talent pipeline amid disruption; COO Reddy: "the goal of the bank for the next 90 days here is to work really well with the teams that we have" and "you're not going to see significant expansion in the number of verticals going forward." Chief Commercial Officer Geisel added: "We've got a lot of inbound calls, and we can be very selective in who we bring on."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral and detail-oriented, concentrating on credit cleanup, loan-growth timing, deposit-cost trajectory, and the sustainability of hiring-driven growth.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive, emphasizing confidence and planning language such as "We are confident" (CEO Lubow) and "we expect" (COO Reddy), while acknowledging limits on deposit-cost declines: "it's going to be challenging" (COO Reddy).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management introduced more incremental operational caveats (day-count-adjusted NIM and a loan-sale loss) while continuing to frame longer-term NIM expansion targets.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The quarter added new strategic items not emphasized previously, including "plans to rebrand Dime at Dime Commercial Bank" (CEO Lubow) and "a new equipment and franchise finance vertical starting May 1" (CEO Lubow).

  • Expense guidance moved higher: "increasing the expense guidance...to approximately $260 million" (COO Reddy) vs. "between $255 million and $257 million" (COO Reddy, Q4 2025).

  • NIM framing shifted to a day-count-adjusted baseline: "run rate NIM...closer to 3.14%" (COO Reddy) vs. "excluding prepayment fees...3.09%" as the prior modeling starting point (COO Reddy, Q4 2025).

  • The loan pipeline increased: "in excess of $1.5 billion" (CEO Lubow) vs. "more than $1.3 billion" (CEO Lubow, Q4 2025).

Risks and Concerns

  • Deposit-cost pressure risk was explicitly acknowledged: "it's going to be challenging for any bank to continue to drop deposit costs" if policy rates remain unchanged (COO Reddy).

  • Credit and resolution activity remained an investor focus, with management emphasizing flexibility to act: "we have the pre-provision earnings power of the bank...to offload relationships and credits" (COO Reddy).

  • Execution risk around growth timing and portfolio reshaping remained central, with management tying inflection to the CRE ratio objective: "reduce our CRE concentration ratio lower to 350%" (COO Reddy).

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 around organic revenue momentum, deposit-cost-driven NIM improvement, and a larger medium-term NIM uplift from back-book repricing, while also flagging near-term items investors may model—an increased 2026 core expense outlook to about $260 million and an expected $2 million negative gain-on-sale impact in Q2 from selling $38 million of loans. The call also broadened the strategic narrative with a planned rebrand to Dime Commercial Bank and the launch of an equipment and franchise finance vertical on May 1, alongside continued hiring focused on deposit gathering and commercial lending, which management said it expects to be accretive to EPS starting in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Immunology drug developer Odyssey seeks U.S. IPO again
data.description:
Odyssey Therapeutics (ODTX), a drug developer focused on immunology, has filed for an initial public offering in the U.S., nearly a year after scrapping its previous IPO plans.

The autoimmune and inflammatory disease specialist filed a Form S-1 registration form with the SEC on Friday, disclosing its intention to sell shares of its common stock on the Nasdaq under the ticker symbol “ODTX.”

In June 2025, the Boston, Massachusetts-based biotech withdrew the Form S-1 registration form filed early last year, noting that it was “not in the best interests of the company to conduct the proposed offering at this time.”

In the refiled S-1, Odyssey (ODTX) didn’t disclose how many shares it plans to offer or the price range it is eyeing. However, the company stated that it will utilize net proceeds from the IPO to fund its operational activities, including the clinical development of its lead asset, OD-001.

The oral inhibitor of receptor-interacting protein kinase 2 (RIPK2) is currently undergoing mid-stage development for ulcerative colitis ((UC)), one of the two main types of an autoimmune disorder known as inflammatory bowel disease.

In H2 2026, the company expects to initiate a Phase 2a trial designed to evaluate OD-001 with Takeda’s (TAK) UC therapy, Entyvio (vedolizumab). The topline data from the randomized trial are expected in H2 2027.

Being a clinical-stage biotech founded in 2021, Odyssey (ODTX) has yet to generate profits. Its net loss widened by ~15% YoY to $148.6M in 2025 as its collaboration revenue, driven by a licensing deal with Terray Therapeutics, fell ~34% YoY to $3.0M.

data.title: First American targets 80%-85% of local branches on Endpoint by end of 2027 amid plan for 2026 record commercial year
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First American Financial Corporation (FAF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We are pleased to report continued momentum in the first quarter, generating adjusted earnings per share of $1.33, a 58% increase from the prior year,” said Mark Seaton (CEO & Director), while framing Commercial as a key driver: “revenue grew 48%, achieving a record for a first quarter,” including “20 orders, generating more than $1 million in premium.”
  • Seaton highlighted mix shifts inside Commercial: “Data centers remain a meaningful tailwind with revenue tied to this sector increasing 76% relative to last year,” and “strong activity in our Energy group, which grew 250% and was a top 5 asset class during the quarter.”
  • On Residential, Seaton said “purchase revenue continues to lag” and added, “purchase revenue declined 4% year-over-year,” while noting refi sensitivity to rates: “we saw a modest benefit during the quarter when mortgage rates dipped into the low 6% range… volumes have since softened as rates have moved higher again.”
  • Seaton described the bank as “a countercyclical earnings driver,” citing deposits and mix: “During Q1, average deposits totaled $6.8 billion, up 19% from last year,” and “29% of deposits came from sources beyond our captive title business, including $1.4 billion from ServiceMac and an additional $300 million from 1031 exchange deposits.”
  • Matthew Wajner (Executive VP & CFO) summarized results and segment revenue: “This quarter, we generated GAAP earnings of $1.21 per diluted share… adjusted earnings… were $1.33 per diluted share,” and “adjusted revenue was $1.7 billion, up 17% compared with the same quarter of 2025.”

Outlook

  • Seaton set expectations for Commercial despite early April order trends: “For the first 3 weeks in April, our opened commercial orders are down 4% relative to last year,” but added, “given our strong pipeline of sizable commercial transactions, we still believe 2026 will be a record year in our commercial business.”
  • On purchase, Seaton reiterated caution: “we remain more cautious than the consensus view,” adding, “So far in April, open purchase orders are down 3% as the sluggish home sale trend continues.”
  • On Endpoint rollout, Seaton said, “We have outlined a plan to scale the platform across First American Title's local branch network by the end of 2027 and we remain on track,” and added, “We expect approximately 80% to 85% of our local branch network to be on Endpoint by the end of next year.”
  • On Sequoia expansion, Seaton said, “By the end of this year, we plan to expand Sequoia across California and Florida with a national rollout planned for 2027.”

Financial Results

  • Wajner detailed Title revenue composition: “Commercial revenue was $271 million,” “purchase revenue was down 4%,” and “Refinance revenue was up 76% compared with last year,” adding that “Refinance accounted for just 8% of our direct revenue this quarter.”
  • On other revenue lines, Wajner said “Information and other revenues were $269 million,” and “Investment income was $154 million in the first quarter, up 12%,” attributing resilience to balances and mix: “shifting its asset mix to fixed income securities, which earn a higher yield and are less sensitive to changes in short-term interest rates.”
  • On expenses and profitability, Wajner said “Personnel costs were $546 million… up 13%,” “Other operating expenses were $277 million… up 13%,” and “Pretax margin in the Title segment was 9.6% or 10.4% on an adjusted basis.”
  • On capital returns, Wajner said the company “repurchase[d] 556,000 shares for a total of $33 million at an average price of $60.21,” and added, “So far in April, we repurchased 296,000 shares for a total of $18 million at an average price of $61.61.”

Q&A

  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank AG: “new entrants leveraging AI… disrupt the title insurance industry” and “ways… to… fend off the competition?” Mark Seaton (CEO & Director): “we're just all in on AI,” and cited moats including “distribution,” “our title plants,” “our balance sheet,” “our data,” and “our technology.”
  • Maxwell Fritscher, Truist: “Commercial ARPO… expectations… balance of ’26?” Seaton: “we still believe 2026 will be a record year for us,” adding “we think that there's going to be a couple more years here of at least strength in commercial market,” and pointed to “data centers” and “energy.”
  • Maxwell Fritscher, Truist: “refinance activity in Canada… expected to be sustained?” Matthew Wajner (Executive VP & CFO): “they don't have the concept of a 30-year fixed rate mortgage… 3- to 5-year,” and “we expect the refi tailwind to continue throughout the year here and into next year for Canada.”
  • Bose George, KBW: “In terms of the escrow deposits… is there more room… at the bank?” Wajner: “we can grow our deposits at the bank… We definitely have more room,” while Seaton added, “the Fed has cut 3x, and yet our investment income is up 12% year-over-year.”
  • Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens: “capital allocation… M&A and buybacks?” Wajner: “we have $248 million remaining on the program,” and “we do acquisitions… but it needs to make sense,” adding, “with our excess capital, we look to give that back to shareholders… through dividends and share buybacks.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to skeptical on competitive threats and sustainability, with pointed framing such as “new entrants leveraging AI to potentially disrupt the title insurance industry” (DeVries, Deutsche Bank AG) and repeated probing on commercial durability and banking/investment income.
  • Management sentiment was positive and assertive on strategy and moats, with Seaton stating “we're just all in on AI” and repeatedly emphasizing “significant advantages,” while also using more cautious language on housing: “Residential purchase revenue continues to lag” and “we remain more cautious than the consensus view.”
  • Versus Q4 2025, management’s tone stayed confident on Commercial and AI rollout timelines; analysts in Q1 2026 focused more directly on AI-driven disruption and defensibility, compared with Q4’s heavier emphasis on margin/tech drag mechanics and regulatory noise.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 leaned harder into AI as an industry battleground, with Seaton saying “we feel like we need to win in our industry with AI,” while Q4 2025 emphasized milestone launches and rollout pacing (Endpoint launch and Sequoia refinance automation) rather than “new entrants” disruption framing.
  • Commercial commentary shifted from Q4’s emphasis that 2026 growth would be “more orders as opposed to ARPO” to Q1’s emphasis that “the fee per file matters more… than the number of orders,” alongside disclosure of “20 orders” above “$1 million in premium.”
  • Capital return messaging became more immediate in Q1 2026 via detailed repurchase activity, after Q4 2025 described buybacks as “opportunistic” without reporting a current-quarter buyback figure.

Risks and Concerns

  • Seaton flagged housing weakness and rate sensitivity: “Residential purchase revenue continues to lag,” and “volumes have since softened as rates have moved higher again.”
  • Competitive and execution risk around AI modernization surfaced in Q&A, with Seaton acknowledging rollout work remaining: “We're at 30% automation rates right now. It's going to take a few years,” while targeting Endpoint “80% to 90%” automation “once it's mature.”
  • Wajner flagged cost pressure directionally through explicit increases in “Personnel costs” and “Other operating expenses,” while highlighting expense discipline via “Our success ratio for the quarter was 58%, which is in line with our target of 60%.”

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 2026 as a mix-driven earnings period led by record first-quarter Commercial performance and higher investment income from growing and diversifying bank deposits, while reiterating caution on residential purchase activity and describing refinance as rate-dependent and still small as a share of direct revenue. The call’s dominant strategic theme was scaling AI platforms (Endpoint and Sequoia) with explicit rollout timelines through 2027 and stated targets for higher automation over time, alongside opportunistic share repurchases as management described the stock “pulled back” while “earnings and outlook have strengthened.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Reliance anticipates Q2 2026 EPS of $5.15 to $5.35 while ramping $2.2B DHS border wall contract
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Reliance, Inc. (RS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Reliance is off to a strong start to 2026, capitalizing on favorable market fundamentals with first quarter volumes, pricing and earnings exceeding our expectations," said (President, CEO & Director Karla Lewis), adding that "our first quarter tons sold were a record" and that non-GAAP EPS was "$5.16."

  • Lewis highlighted contract wins: "we also secured 2 significant government contracts in the first quarter to supply the Department of Homeland Security border wall and Joint Strike Fighter projects through our AMI Metals wholly owned subsidiary," and said they "collectively represent up to approximately $3 billion in revenue." She also noted timing: "our first quarter results did not include any contributions from the border wall contract."

  • On capital returns and spending, Lewis said, "our full year 2026 outlook for capital expenditures is approximately $300 million," and added, "we increased our dividend rate by 4% to an annualized $5 per share and repurchased $234 million of our shares."

  • (Executive VP & COO Stephen Koch) tied performance to shipments and pricing: "Record tons sold increased 9.4% from the fourth quarter of 2025," and said Reliance "significantly outperform[ed] the service center industry" as "tons sold increased 2.7%" year-over-year while the industry "reported a decline of 5.1%."

  • (CFO & Senior VP Arthur Ajemyan) framed Q1 profitability and key accounting headwinds: "We delivered a strong first quarter with sales up 15% year-over-year," and said "higher-than-anticipated material costs resulted in the first quarter LIFO expense of $37.5 million... prompting us to raise our full year LIFO outlook to $150 million from the prior $100 million annual estimate."

Outlook

  • Ajemyan guided to Q2 earnings: "We anticipate second quarter 2026 non-GAAP earnings per diluted share in the range of $5.15 to $5.35," and said this includes "an estimated $37.5 million of LIFO expense or about $0.54 per diluted share."

  • Management reiterated demand/pricing expectations and flagged external variables: Ajemyan said, "we expect both demand and pricing to remain healthy in the second quarter of 2026, generally in line with Q1," while noting "ongoing risks from domestic and international trade policy and the conflict in the Middle East."

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guide, the company’s Q2 EPS range followed Q4’s guidance language that pricing would improve into Q1; in Q4, Ajemyan had said, "we anticipate first quarter 2026 non-GAAP earnings per diluted share in the range of $4.50 to $4.70." In Q1, Lewis also emphasized that Q2 margin upside from price increases is expected to be lower than Q1: "We expect some continued price improvement in Q2, but not to the level of Q1."

Financial results

  • Ajemyan reported profitability and margins: "Our gross profit of $1.2 billion was up 23% compared to the fourth quarter of 2025," and "non-GAAP FIFO gross profit margin expanded to 30.1% compared to 28.5% in the fourth quarter of 2025."

  • On operating leverage and costs, Ajemyan said "non-GAAP SG&A expense increased 6% compared to the first quarter of 2025," while "non-GAAP pretax income" rose "to $354 million with an 8.8% pretax income margin."

  • Cash flow and balance sheet items emphasized capital allocation flexibility: Ajemyan said "cash flow from operations in the first quarter was approximately $151 million," and that the company repurchased stock "at an average price of $299 per share" with "approximately $529 million remaining" under the program; he added, "as of March 31, our total debt was $1.7 billion" and "net debt-to-EBITDA ratio" was "1."

Q&A

  • Martin Englert, Seaport Research Partners: Asked if Q2 implied FIFO gross margins were conservative and what to consider given pricing and the DHS contract; (President, CEO & Director Lewis) responded, "Q1 was a good strong pricing environment" but "we expect some continued price improvement in Q2, but not to the level of Q1," and added border wall "gross profit margins will bring our consolidated number down a bit" while "extremely low operating cost" should support earnings.

  • Bennett Moore, JPMorgan: Asked about border wall cadence and pricing structure; Lewis said, "we began shipping this month in April" and expects activity "to increase as we move into Q3 and beyond," while adding, "we can't get into the specifics on the pricing."

  • Moore, JPMorgan: Pressed on aluminum tariffs and LIFO; Lewis said the company can pass through the "50% tariff" but "we're not necessarily getting a full margin on that 50% tariff cost," calling the impact "a bit of a drag" while noting "significantly higher gross profit dollars." Ajemyan added aluminum gross profit dollars were up "almost 17%, 18%" year-over-year and said last year "nearly half" of LIFO was aluminum-related, while this year it is "a little less than half, maybe over 1/3."

  • Samuel McKinney, KeyBanc: Asked if sequential gross margin expansion reflects improved aluminum navigation; Lewis answered, "I think that's fair," and said the company has "made progress" pushing through costs.

  • Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about acquisition pipeline and capital allocation; Lewis said the pipeline is "pretty consistent" and "no change in our appetite," adding the company has not had to choose among "acquisitions," "grow organically," dividends, and "share repurchases."

  • Bennett Moore, JPMorgan: Asked about semiconductor share opportunities and qualification timing; Lewis said activity has improved at "semiconductor chip equipment manufacturers" and described Reliance’s footprint (including "Singapore"), while (Executive VP & COO Koch) added, "we're already certified with them and already picking up some business."

  • Englert, Seaport Research Partners: Asked for DHS tonnage details; Lewis said, "we have not disclosed tonnage," but stated the "total is $2.2 billion" and that "Phase 1 is $1.4 billion" running through "mid-2027" (Ajemyan) and "end of Q2, 2027" (Lewis).

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but detail-seeking, focusing on the durability of margins under tariff-driven cost inflation and contract mix; Englert asked if guidance was "being conservative," and Moore repeatedly pressed for contract cadence, pricing protection, and aluminum/LIFO specifics.

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A when discussing margin normalization and tariffs; Lewis repeatedly emphasized limits on disclosure ("we can't get into the specifics") and framed aluminum effects as temporary ("We think that's transitory"), while maintaining confidence on demand ("we... feel really good about 2026 across demand across most of the products and end markets we're selling into").

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from expecting pricing to improve into Q1 to describing Q1 as unusually strong and Q2 as steadier; in Q4, Ajemyan said "pricing for most products will improve in the first quarter of 2026," while in Q1 Lewis said Q2 price improvement should be "not to the level of Q1."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call introduced execution and timing detail on the DHS border wall program, including that shipments began "in April" and are expected to rise into "Q3," while also stating Q1 results had "no contributions" from the contract; the prior call did not discuss these government contract awards.

  • Guidance framing evolved from Q4’s Q1 EPS range of "$4.50 to $4.70" to Q1’s Q2 EPS range of "$5.15 to $5.35," alongside an explicit increase in expected annual LIFO expense to "$150 million" from "$100 million."

  • Analyst focus stayed consistent on gross margins, tariffs/aluminum dynamics, and demand visibility; however, the current quarter added heavier emphasis on contract economics (mix dilution vs. earnings leverage) and the LIFO interplay with tariff-driven aluminum costs.

Risks and concerns

  • Ajemyan cited macro and geopolitical risks: Q2 expectations are "subject to ongoing risks from domestic and international trade policy and the conflict in the Middle East."

  • Management emphasized tariff-driven volatility in aluminum margins and accounting impacts; Lewis said the company is not getting "a full margin" on the tariff pass-through and described LIFO as "a double hit" when tariffs raise costs.

  • Supply tightness was flagged as both opportunity and constraint; Koch said, "Hot-rolled coil is on limited availability," while Lewis noted "extending lead times" can support pricing and make "access to metal... a strategic advantage."

Final takeaway

Reliance management described Q1 as a stronger-than-expected quarter with record tons sold, expanding FIFO margin, and non-GAAP EPS of $5.16, while positioning Q2 for similar demand and pricing with EPS guided to $5.15 to $5.35. Executives emphasized two major government contract awards—especially the DHS border wall program beginning shipments in April and expected to ramp into Q3—while acknowledging that contract mix and aluminum tariffs can pressure consolidated margin percentage even as gross profit dollars rise. The company also raised its full-year LIFO outlook to $150 million and reiterated capital returns through a higher dividend and continued share repurchases alongside roughly $300 million of planned 2026 capital expenditures.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. stocks slide as conflict in the Strait of Hormuz intensifies
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were significantly lower on Thursday, a day after the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) reached records, as investors digested new Middle East conflict developments. 

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.6%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.7%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 3 basis points higher at 4.34%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 3.84%.

U.S. President Donald Trump has ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Previously, he had said negotiation could take place once again as soon as Friday, according to The New York Post.

On the other hand, Iran claimed to have seized two ships in the Strait of Hormuz that crossed “without authorization,” according to state news agency Tasnim.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) surged to $95, while Brent (CO1:COM) was also higher at $105 per barrel.

On the earnings front, Tesla (TSLA) was last -3.4% after investors factored in Elon Musk’s warning of heavy capital expenditure spending.

Initial jobless claims for last week rose beyond consensus, according to the U.S. Department of Labor. In addition, the AAII survey indicated that bullish sentiment among individual investors surged, while bearish views declined.

Moreover, the S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI index came in at 52.0 in April, vs. 50.5 consensus and 50.3 recorded in March, preliminary data released by S&P Global showed.
